query: Apoprotein B-48 presence in chylomicrons VLDL LDL HDL

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Lipoprotein Metabolism -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Triglycerides get packaged into the chylomicron through its interaction with the apoB48, the backbone apolipoprotein. ApoC-II and E are acquired from HDL as the chylomicron circulates. A cholesteryl ester transfer protein (CETP) promotes the transfer of cholesteryl esters from HDL to apoB containing lipoproteins, including VLDL, VLDL remnant, IDL, and LDL in exchange for triglycerides. As a result, HDL cholesterol decreases, and cholesterol content in VLDL increases. [5] Subsequently, it returns to the liver and is endocytosed by scavenger receptors on hepatocytes. HDL can then be excreted or recycled by the Golgi. [6] Apo-B-48 allows lipid binding to the chylomicron from circulation before lipoprotein lipase (LPL) hydrolyzes the core triglycerides and releases fatty acids. The apo C-II is a cofactor for LPL making the chylomicron smaller with each reaction. As the chylomicron becomes metabolized, the chylomicron remnant forms and ultimately cleared by hepatic chylomicron remnant receptors. The apoE on the chylomicron remnant acts as a high-affinity ligand to signal the hepatic chylomicron remnant receptor. The surface constituents from the chylomicron remnant get transferred to form HDL. When LPL is deficient, termed chylomicronemia, patients can have severely elevated triglyceride levels exceeding 2000mg/dl, potentially leading to pancreatitis. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Very Low Density Lipoprotein -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The impact of VLDL on cardiovascular diseases is mainly correlated to its role in atherosclerosis and coronary disease. [26] The accumulation of triglyceride-predominant lipoproteins such as chylomicrons and VLDL strongly contributes to the rupture of atherosclerotic plaques. Additionally, VLDL has been associated with peripheral artery disease, leading to occlusive limb events, vascular stiffness, and carotid artery thickness. [27] Besides the lipidic components of VLDL, its apolipoprotein composition also directly affects the development of cardiovascular disorders. Studies have shown that higher Apo-B and lower Apo C-III concentrations are associated with higher cardiovascular risk. [28] [29] This leads to the hypothesis that Apo-B is one of the major drivers in the development of atherosclerosis. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Very Low Density Lipoprotein -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: The composition of VLDL consists of approximately 90% lipids and 10% proteins. This proportion gives VLDL a low density of 0.96 to 1.006 g/ml, hence its name. Of its total lipid component, triglycerides take up to 70% of VLDL’s mass, with cholesterol esters and fatty acids making up the rest. [5] Regarding its lower protein component, VLDL is associated with multiple apolipoproteins: Apo B-100, Apo C-I, Apo C-II, Apo C-III, and Apo E. [6] Apolipoproteins are cofactors for numerous enzymes throughout the body, ligands for surface receptors, and essential structural elements. The biological functions and metabolism of VLDL are strongly related to these apolipoproteins, as well as VLDL’s potential role in disease development. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Dyslipidemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Phenotype I is an abnormality of chylomicrons and will result in triglycerides greater than 99 percentiles. Phenotype IIa consists mainly of LDL cholesterol abnormality and will have a total cholesterol concentration greater than 90 percentile and possibly apolipoprotein B greater than 90 percentile. Phenotype IIb consists of abnormality in LDL and VLDL cholesterol. This type will result in total cholesterol or triglycerides greater than the 90 percentile and apolipoprotein greater than the 90 percentile. Phenotype III is an abnormality in VLDL remnants and chylomicrons, which results in elevated total cholesterol and triglycerides greater than 90 percentile. Phenotype IV is mainly when VLDL is abnormal and results in total cholesterol greater than 90 percentile. This type can also present with triglycerides greater than 90 percentile and low HDL. Phenotype V is when chylomicrons and VLDL are abnormal and triglycerides are greater than 99 percentiles. [6] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Very Low Density Lipoprotein -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The lipid transport pathway starts in the small intestine when the dietary lipids (mainly triglycerides) are packaged into chylomicrons utilizing Apo B-48 as its backbone structure. Chylomicrons are then secreted into the lymphatic system to later travel throughout the systemic circulation. As they travel throughout the body, chylomicrons obtain Apo C-II and Apo-E from circulating HDL. The recently acquired Apo C-II acts as an activating cofactor for lipoprotein lipase (LPL), which hydrolyzes the triglycerides found in the chylomicrons’ core and allows it to release free fatty acids to the peripheral tissues. As chylomicrons lose their triglycerides by the action of peripheral LPL, they transform into chylomicron remnants. These remnants use their Apo-E as high-affinity ligands to bind to hepatic receptors and internalize into the liver. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Very Low Density Lipoprotein -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: In the hepatocytes, triglycerides and cholesterol esters are transferred to Apo B-100 by the action of the microsomal triglyceride transfer protein (MTTP). This lipidated Apo B-100 is now called nascent VLDL and is secreted to the circulation. [7] [8] Like chylomicrons, Nascent VLDL obtains Apo C-II and Apo-E from circulating HDL and is now termed mature VLDL. Mature VLDL transports endogenous lipids from the liver to the peripheral adipose, cardiac, and muscle tissues (unlike chylomicrons, which carry exogenous dietary lipids from the small intestine). [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Apolipoprotein B -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The assessment of cardiovascular disease has, for the last several decades, had its basis in the findings of the Framingham Heart Study cohorts with emphasis on total cholesterol and low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C). [3] [1] Apo B is a component of all atherogenic or potentially atherogenic particles, including small very-low-density lipoprotein (VLDL), intermediate-density lipoprotein (IDL), LDL, and lipoprotein(a) [Lp(a)], and each particle contains 1 molecule of apo B. Therefore, apo B provides a direct measure of the number of atherogenic lipoprotein particles in circulation.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Chylomicron -- Cellular Level -- Apolipoproteins:
retrieved_docs.content: Apolipoprotein C-II is another component that both VLDL and chylomicrons acquire from HDLs. ApoC-II plays a significant role in the metabolism of triglycerides since it is a cofactor for the pivotal enzyme in TG metabolism, lipoprotein lipase (LPL). [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Chylomicron -- Cellular Level -- Apolipoproteins:
retrieved_docs.content: Apolipoprotein E is another component derived from HDL that facilitates the clearance of chylomicron remnants by the liver. While ApoAs are present in chylomicrons, their roles are not well defined in chylomicron metabolism, except for apo -A5, which activates LPL.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Low Density Lipoprotein -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: The density of the lipoproteins is directly proportional to the protein content. Chylomicrons mobilize dietary lipids from the intestine to other tissues. They are the largest in size among the lipoproteins, are the least dense, and comprise about 80% of triacylglycerols. They are assembled in the enterocytes and via lacteals enter the systemic circulation. The apolipoproteins of chylomicrons include apoB-48, apoE, and apoC-II. Chylomicrons carry dietary fatty acids to various tissues, where they will be used for energy or storage. The remnants of chylomicrons containing cholesterol, apoE, and apoB-48, are cleared by the liver via remnant receptors. [5] [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Very Low Density Lipoprotein -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The Apo C-II in mature VLDL activates LPL in the capillary endothelium of tissues to cleave their stored triglycerides into 1 monoglyceride and 2 free fatty acids, releasing them from the VLDL and allowing them to be delivered to the tissues to be used for storage or energy production. [9] [10] Insulin also upregulates Apo C-II's activity and increases triglyceride hydrolysis into free fatty acids for tissue absorption. Simultaneously, the cholesterol ester transfer protein (CETP) promotes the exchange of VLDL’s triglycerides and phospholipids for HDL’s cholesterol esters. After releasing enough triglycerides either into the tissues or to HDL, the core of the VLDL gets reduced, and the VLDL remnant is now called IDL. Approximately half of the circulating IDL is then taken by the liver by binding to the Apo B-100 and Apo-E. The remaining half of the IDL loses its Apo-E and transforms into LDL through the action of the hepatic lipase.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Apolipoprotein B -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Lipids such as cholesterol are insoluble in plasma, and for delivery to tissues such as the adrenal gland, gonads, etc., they have to be packaged into lipoproteins with cholesterol esters and triglycerides in the core and phospholipids, free cholesterol, and apolipoproteins on the surface. Such is the composition of the lipoproteins. [1] Apolipoprotein B (ApoB) is the primary apolipoprotein and is the carrier for the following lipids: chylomicrons, low-density lipoprotein ( LDL), very low-density lipoprotein (VLDL), intermediate-density lipoprotein (IDL), and lipoprotein (a).  ApoB is not found in high-density lipoproteins (HDL). The latter are reconstituted into lipoproteins with Apo A.  Hepatic ApoB has a molecular mass of 540000 Da.  There are two circulating forms of Apo B, Apo B48 (from the small intestine) and Apo B100 (from the liver). [1] Intestinal ApoB, which is present in chylomicrons, has a molecular mass of 48% of that of hepatic ApoB.  Hence, hepatic ApoB is termed ApoB100, and intestinal Apo B as apoB 48. The same gene codes for both ApoB48 and ApoB 100.  Apo B-100 contains 4536 amino acids and is necessary for the assembly of VLDL in the liver and also serves as the primary ligand for LDL receptor-mediated clearance of LDL particles from the blood. Apo B-48 has 2512 amino acids and is essential for the formation of chylomicrons, and serves in the absorption of dietary fats from the intestine.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertriglyceridemia -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Lipoprotein lipase deficiency, Apo C II deficiency, Apo AV homozygosity, and glycosylphosphatidylinositol-anchored HDL-binding protein 1 (GPIHBP1) mutations all present with chylomicronemia. [7] Syndromes presenting with hypertriglyceridemia: Familial combined hyperlipidemia is marked by elevated VLDL and or LDL. The phenotype varies from isolated hypertriglyceridemia to isolated hypercholesterolemia. Regardless, increased apo B concentration is seen and is used to distinguish from familial hypertriglyceridemia. It is associated with premature cardiovascular disease. [7] Familial hypertriglyceridemia is more common and is associated with the secretion of large VLDL particles, typically with low LDL and HDL. There is increased VLDL production and decreased VLDL catabolism, resulting in saturation of lipoprotein Lipase. It has an autosomal dominant inheritance. It is usually not associated with the premature cardiovascular disease unless the TGL levels are very high but can present with pancreatitis. [7] Familial dysbetalipoproteinemia (type III hyperlipoproteinemia) is due to defective Apo E. It is characterized by the build-up of chylomicrons and VLDL remnants. Elevation in both cholesterol and triglycerides and roughly similar levels should raise suspicion for this syndrome. It is associated with premature cardiovascular disease and peripheral vascular disease. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Chylomicron -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The backbone structural protein is the truncated apolipoprotein B-48, which is the main non-exchangeable protein. However, it does contain other apolipoproteins, including ApoA1, A2, A3, A5 C2, C3, and ApoE. [2] Chylomicrons have a density below 0.94 g/ml and remain at the origin of lipoprotein electrophoresis. [1] Their major lipid is triglycerides, which comprise more than 75% of the particle, and they have the lowest protein content of all lipoproteins of around 2 percent, explaining why they have such low density on ultracentrifugation.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, High Density Lipoprotein -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The liver can also uptake HDL via its apolipoprotein Apo-E. Apo-E interacts with the LDL receptor and leads to the uptake of the entire LDL particle. The SR-B1 pathway is responsible for the uptake of HDL cholesterol into the liver without the internalization of the HDL particle. After cholesterol uptake, the smaller HDL particle gets released back into the circulation. SR-B1 deficiency results in increased HDL levels but correlates with an increased, not decreased, risk of atherosclerosis. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Intermediate Density Lipoprotein -- Clinical Significance -- Dysbetalipoproteinemia
retrieved_docs.content: Dysbetalipoproteinemia, or type III hyperlipoproteinemia by Fredrickson, Levy, and Lees' classification, is a rare condition with a prevalence of 1 in 1,000 to 1 in 5,000. This disorder is associated with the production of the ApoE2 (Arg158Cys) isoform, which has a reduced affinity for LDL receptors and LDL receptor-related proteins, impairing hepatic clearance of IDL particles and chylomicron remnants. This impairment results in the accumulation of β-VLDL and IDL1. [43] LDL cholesterol levels may be low due to reduced conversion of IDL to LDL.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1880
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism constitute a broad category of metabolic conditions affecting the processing and regulation of fats in the body, and hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية) is a specific disorder within this category. Hyperlipoproteinemia is characterized by abnormally high levels of lipoproteins in the blood, reflecting a particular manifestation of the underlying disturbances in lipid metabolism. Recognizing hyperlipoproteinemia as a subset of disorders of lipid metabolism underscores its role in the spectrum of lipid‑related diseases and informs targeted diagnostic and therapeutic approaches.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1225
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin are all classified as members of the therapeutic group “HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs.” This class of agents inhibits the enzyme HMG CoA reductase, thereby reducing hepatic cholesterol synthesis and lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels. As plain lipid‑modifying drugs, these statins are used to manage hyperlipidaemia and reduce cardiovascular risk in patients with elevated cholesterol. Recognizing that fluvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin share this mechanism of action and therapeutic category helps clinicians select appropriate statin therapy based on individual drug properties, efficacy, and safety profiles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2538
retrieved_docs.content: Inclisiran and evolocumab are both classified as members of the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “Other lipid modifying agents.” This classification indicates that each drug is used to modify lipid concentrations, particularly low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol, and is employed in the treatment of hyperlipidaemia and the reduction of cardiovascular risk. By belonging to the same ATC category, inclisiran and evolocumab share a therapeutic purpose of lowering lipid levels, although they may differ in their specific mechanisms of action and dosing regimens. Recognizing them as part of the “Other lipid modifying agents” group helps clinicians understand their role within the broader spectrum of lipid‑lowering therapies and supports informed decision‑making in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1214
retrieved_docs.content: Autosomal dominant inheritance is the mode of transmission for فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ (hyperlipidemia type IIa). This genetic pattern means that a single copy of the altered gene inherited from either parent is sufficient to cause the disorder. فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ is characterized by markedly elevated low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels, which increase the risk of premature atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Because the condition follows an autosomal dominant pattern, it often appears in successive generations, and first‑degree relatives of an affected individual have a 50 % chance of inheriting the same lipid abnormality. Recognizing the autosomal dominant inheritance of فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ is essential for family screening, early diagnosis, and implementation of lipid‑lowering therapies to reduce cardiovascular risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2462
retrieved_docs.content: HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid modifying drugs constitute a therapeutic class of statin medications that lower cholesterol by inhibiting the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme in the hepatic cholesterol synthesis pathway. Atorvastatin, fluvastatin, and pravastatin are individual agents that belong to this class. By reducing low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and modestly increasing high‑density lipoprotein (HDL), these drugs are employed to manage hyperlipidemia and to lower the risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Their shared mechanism of action and lipid‑modifying effects make them central components of preventive cardiology and patient care strategies aimed at controlling dyslipidemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2216
retrieved_docs.content: Medium chain triglycerides are contraindicated in patients who have ارتفاع نسبة الدهون الثلاثية في الدم (elevated blood triglyceride levels), indicating that the use of medium chain triglycerides should be avoided in individuals with this condition because it may exacerbate or is not recommended for managing high triglyceride concentrations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1191
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, and cerivastatin are all members of the class of HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. As HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, these agents act by blocking the rate‑limiting enzyme in cholesterol biosynthesis, leading to reduced low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels and a lower risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Their classification as plain lipid‑modifying drugs underscores their primary therapeutic role in managing dyslipidaemia without additional pharmacologic actions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1001
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, fluvastatin, and cerivastatin are all members of the class of HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, which are classified as plain lipid‑modifying drugs. As HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, these agents act by blocking the rate‑limiting enzyme in cholesterol biosynthesis, leading to reduced low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels and a lower risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Their inclusion in the plain lipid‑modifying drug category underscores their primary role in managing hyperlipidemia and preventing related cardiovascular events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 4 -- Community 2847
retrieved_docs.content: Colestipol belongs to the class of bile acid sequestrants, a group of medications that bind bile acids in the gastrointestinal tract to lower low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and are employed in the treatment of hyperlipidemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 848
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, rosuvastatin, and cerivastatin are all members of the therapeutic class known as HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This class of agents works by inhibiting the enzyme HMG‑CoA reductase, a key step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis, thereby lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and overall plasma lipid levels. As plain lipid‑modifying drugs, these statins are commonly prescribed to reduce cardiovascular risk in patients with hypercholesterolemia, atherosclerotic disease, or other dyslipidemias, and they form a cornerstone of lipid‑lowering therapy in contemporary medical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1212
retrieved_docs.content: Stofwisselingsstoornissen van lipoproteïnen en overige lipidemieën, a broad classification of metabolic disorders affecting lipoproteins and other lipid abnormalities, includes the specific condition فرط ليبيدات الدم, which denotes hyperlipidemia characterized by elevated blood lipid levels. Recognizing فرط ليبيدات الدم as a child entity within this larger category helps clinicians organize lipid disorders and guide appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2780
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism are a specific category of metabolic disorder (اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي) and are also classified as hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية). These conditions involve abnormalities in the synthesis, transport, or breakdown of lipids, leading to elevated blood lipid levels and associated risks such as atherosclerosis and cardiovascular disease. Recognizing disorders of lipid metabolism as both a metabolic disturbance and a form of hyperlipoproteinemia underscores their role in systemic metabolic health and guides targeted diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Fenofibrát is classified as a member of the “Fibrates, lipid modifying drugs, plain” group, indicating that it belongs to the fibrate class of plain lipid‑modifying agents. As a fibrate, fenofibrát shares the typical pharmacologic actions of this drug family, which are used to alter lipid profiles by lowering triglyceride levels and modestly increasing high‑density lipoprotein cholesterol.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 779
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, and cerivastatin are all members of the class HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This drug class works by inhibiting the enzyme HMG‑CoA reductase, the rate‑limiting step in cholesterol biosynthesis, which leads to reductions in low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and triglycerides and modest increases in high‑density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol. As plain lipid‑modifying agents, these statins are widely used to treat hyperlipidemia and to lower the risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Recognizing that atorvastatin, fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, and cerivastatin belong to the same pharmacologic group aids clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy, anticipating class‑related benefits, and monitoring for shared adverse effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1978
retrieved_docs.content: Bezafibrate is classified as a member of the fibrates, a group of plain lipid‑modifying drugs. As part of this drug class, bezafibrate shares the typical pharmacologic actions of fibrates, which are used to alter lipid profiles by lowering triglyceride levels and modestly increasing high‑density lipoprotein cholesterol.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 414
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin, rosuvastatin and pravastatin are members of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, a group classified as plain lipid‑modifying drugs that are used to lower cholesterol levels.
